original_hypothesis.title: Forger ForgePotential TOCTOU: Year Reset Between Listing and Forging Bypasses Limit
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: forgeWithListed does not re-validate the forger's forgePotential at forge time. It only increments forgingCounts[forgerTokenId] without checking against forgePotential. The check was done at listing time in listForForging, but _resetForgingCountIfNeeded is called in forgeWithListed which can reset forgingCounts to 0 if a year has passed. This creates a TOCTOU: forger lists at count=forgePotential (allowed by off-by-one), a year passes, forgeWithListed resets count to 0 and increments to 1. The forger can repeat this cycle indefinitely, forging unlimited times with a single listing per year cycle.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The comment in forgeWithListed says 'already checked in listForForging' but the state can change between listing and forging due to year reset.

proof_of_signal.pattern: forgingCounts[forgerTokenId]++ without re-checking against forgePotential; preceded by _resetForgingCountIfNeeded which may reset count to 0

preconditions: Forger token is listed for forging
One year (365 days by default) passes between listing and forging
A merger token owner executes forgeWithListed

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Listing persists indefinitely; forgePotential check at listing time becomes stale after year reset
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Each year boundary creates new forge opportunities for stale listings

operational_questions: Do listings expire or have a time-to-live?
How common are listings that persist across year boundaries?
validation_requirements: List forger at max count, wait 1 year, forge — verify reset enables additional forge

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Own forger token, exhaust forgePotential, list at max count
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Have merger call forgeWithListed — _resetForgingCountIfNeeded resets to 0, then increments to 1
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat annually for unlimited forges from a single token
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Need merger partner; 1 year wait per cycle

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Forging limits become meaningless over time
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires code update to add forge-time validation

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Forging count resets for listed tokens
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Token forging well beyond its forgePotential over multiple years
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Annual forging patterns from long-lived listings

validation_result.mechanism: TOCTOU: missing forgePotential re-validation at forge time
validation_result.impact_summary: forgeWithListed skips forgePotential check for the forger, relying on stale listing-time validation. Year-based reset between listing and forging changes state, making the original check meaningless. Practical exploitation impact is limited but the code defect is clear.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis correctly identifies that forgeWithListed does not re-validate the forger's forgePotential at forge time. The code at lines ~107-112 explicitly skips the check with the comment 'already checked in listForForging for the forger'. However, _resetForgingCountIfNeeded is called in forgeWithListed BEFORE the increment, which can reset forgingCounts[forgerTokenId] to 0 if a year has passed. This creates a genuine TOCTOU: the check in listForForging validated the forging count against forgePotential at listing time, but the state (forgingCounts) can change between listing and forging due to the year reset.

The practical impact is somewhat limited because: (1) each forgeWithListed call cancels the listing, requiring re-listing for additional forges, and (2) after a year reset, the forger could simply re-list with a fresh count=0 anyway, achieving the same result. However, the stale listing allows a forge to proceed without the forger needing to actively re-list, and the missing check is a genuine code defect regardless of exploitation profitability.

The defect is compounded by the off-by-one bug (forgingCounts <= forgePotential should be < forgePotential), allowing listing at the exact limit. A forger at count=forgePotential can list (passes due to <=), wait a year, and the forge proceeds with count reset to 0.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies the vulnerable pattern. In EntityForging.sol's forgeWithListed function, the sequence is: (1) _resetForgingCountIfNeeded(forgerTokenId) which may set forgingCounts to 0, then (2) forgingCounts[forgerTokenId]++ without any check against forgePotential. The merger IS checked (forgingCounts[mergerTokenId] <= mergerForgePotential), proving asymmetric treatment. The code comment explicitly acknowledges the missing check with incorrect reasoning.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: forgingCounts[forgerTokenId]++ is performed without checking against forgePotential. _resetForgingCountIfNeeded immediately before this line can reset the count to 0 if a year has passed, making the listing-time check in listForForging stale. The merger gets proper validation (checked after increment), but the forger does not.
This function resets forgingCounts to 0 when a year has passed. When called in forgeWithListed, it invalidates the forgePotential check that was done in listForForging, creating the TOCTOU window.

The forgePotential check uses <= instead of < (off-by-one), and this is the ONLY place where forger's forgePotential is validated. Since listings have no expiration and forgingCounts can be reset between listing and forging, this check becomes stale.

calling_sequence: 1. Forger calls listForForging(tokenId, fee) — _resetForgingCountIfNeeded called, forgePotential checked (forgingCounts <= forgePotential), listing created
5. forgingCounts[forgerTokenId]++ increments to 1 WITHOUT re-checking against forgePotential
6. Forge proceeds, new token minted, listing cancelled
7. Forger can re-list (count=1, passes listing check), repeat cycle
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The TOCTOU occurs between listForForging (where forgePotential is checked) and forgeWithListed (where it is NOT checked). The state change occurs in _resetForgingCountIfNeeded called within forgeWithListed at the beginning of the function. The forgingCounts variable is reset to 0, making the original listing-time check meaningless. The code then increments forgingCounts without any validation against forgePotential, only relying on the stale check from listing time. This is evidenced by the explicit comment in the code: 'do not check forge potential here as it is already checked in listForForging for the forger' — this reasoning is incorrect because _resetForgingCountIfNeeded changes the very state that was validated.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No config dependency. The oneYearInDays defaults to 365 days. Deployment script does not modify this value.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is valid but overstated in impact. Concrete scenario: (1) Forger with forgePotential=1 lists at forgingCounts=1 (passes due to off-by-one: 1<=1). (2) 365 days pass without anyone calling forgeWithListed. (3) Merger calls forgeWithListed — _resetForgingCountIfNeeded resets count to 0, then increment to 1. Forge succeeds. (4) Listing cancelled. (5) Forger re-lists (count=1, 1<=1 passes). Cycle repeats. However, the same forging cycle would occur if the forger simply waited for the year reset and re-listed fresh. The stale listing saves one re-listing step but doesn't increase total forges per year beyond what the year reset already enables.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The code defect is real: forgePotential is not re-validated at forge time, and the code comment explaining the omission is incorrect. The asymmetric treatment (merger is checked, forger is not) further confirms this is unintentional. However, the practical impact is LIMITED because: (1) each forge consumes the listing (cancelled), (2) the year reset allows fresh listing with count=0 anyway, (3) the total forges per year cycle remains forgePotential+1 (due to off-by-one) regardless of stale listings. The TOCTOU enables the same number of forges achievable through normal re-listing after year reset, making exploitation economically equivalent to normal usage.
recommended_mitigations: Re-validate forger's forgePotential in forgeWithListed after _resetForgingCountIfNeeded: add `uint8 forgerForgePotential = uint8((nftContract.getTokenEntropy(forgerTokenId) / 10) % 10); require(forgerForgePotential > 0 && forgingCounts[forgerTokenId] < forgerForgePotential, 'Forger limit reached');` before the increment
Fix the off-by-one in listForForging: change `forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential` to `forgingCounts[tokenId] < forgePotential`
Consider adding listing expiration (time-to-live) to prevent stale listings persisting across year boundaries
Apply consistent validation pattern: check forgePotential after increment (not before) using strict less-than-or-equal for both forger and merger, matching the merger's validation pattern
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy all contracts. 2. Mint two tokens: one forger (entropy%3==0) with forgePotential=1, one merger. 3. Call listForForging for the forger. 4. Call forgeWithListed to use up the forge (count→1). 5. Re-list forger (passes due to off-by-one: 1<=1). 6. Advance time by 365 days. 7. Call forgeWithListed — observe count reset to 0 then increment to 1. 8. Verify forge succeeds without any forgePotential check at forge time. 9. Compare: if forgePotential check were added in forgeWithListed, would the forge still succeed? (Yes, because count was reset to 0, so 1<=1 passes).
uncertainties: The practical exploitation impact may be negligible since the year reset enables fresh listing anyway, achieving the same forge count
The hypothesis claims 'unlimited forges' but each forge consumes the listing, requiring re-listing which re-validates
The exact line numbers in proof_of_signal (107-112) may vary depending on formatting; the pattern is confirmed in the forgeWithListed function body
additional_testing_needed: Verify that no other code path cancels stale listings (e.g., time-based expiration not visible in provided code)
Test edge case: forger with forgePotential=0 somehow getting listed (should be impossible due to forgePotential > 0 check)
Quantify actual extra forges enabled by TOCTOU vs normal year-reset re-listing to determine true impact delta
